section.title: The Binding of Isaac
section.sectionRationale: This is the climb of Abraham's faith, the fiercest test in Genesis. God asks for the son of promise, Abraham obeys, and at the last moment God Himself provides a substitute. The scene reaches forward, on the same mountain range, to another Father and another Son.
section.thesis: God tests Abraham to the very edge, and Abraham trusts that God will provide; the ram caught in the thicket dies in Isaac's place, and God renews His oath to bless the whole world through the seed of the obedient son.
section.frameNote: Five scenes: the command; the journey of obedience; the binding and the stayed hand; the oath renewed; and the family of Nahor, which quietly introduces Rebekah.

units.label: The command to sacrifice
units.note: take your son, your only son

verses.text: Some time later God {{tested|tested}} Abraham and said to him, “Abraham!” “Here I am,” he answered.
“Take your son,” God said, “your only son Isaac, whom you love, and go to the land of Moriah. Offer him there as a burnt offering on one of the mountains, which I will show you.”

So Abraham got up early the next morning, saddled his donkey, and took along two of his servants and his son Isaac. He split the wood for a {{burntoffering|burnt offering}} and set out for the place God had designated.
On the third day Abraham looked up and saw the place in the distance.
“Stay here with the donkey,” Abraham told his servants. “The boy and I will go over there to worship, and then we will return to you.”
Abraham took the wood for the burnt offering and placed it on his son Isaac. He himself carried the fire and the sacrificial knife, and the two of them walked on together.
Then Isaac said to his father Abraham, “My father!” “Here I am, my son,” he replied. “The fire and the wood are here,” said Isaac, “but where is the lamb for the burnt offering?”
Abraham answered, “God Himself will {{provide|provide}} the lamb for the burnt offering, my son.” And the two walked on together.

units.label: The binding and the stayed hand
units.note: the ram in place of his son

verses.text: When they arrived at the place God had designated, Abraham built the altar there and arranged the wood. He bound his son Isaac and placed him on the altar, atop the wood.
Then Abraham reached out his hand and took the knife to slaughter his son.
Just then the angel of the LORD called out to him from heaven, “Abraham, Abraham!” “Here I am,” he replied.
“Do not lay a hand on the boy or do anything to him,” said the angel, “for now I know that you {{fear|fear}} God, since you have not withheld your only son from me.”
Then Abraham looked up and saw behind him a ram in a thicket, caught by its horns. So he went and took the ram and offered it as a burnt offering in place of his son.
And Abraham called that place The LORD Will Provide. So to this day it is said, “On the mountain of the LORD it will be provided.”

units.label: The oath renewed
units.note: in your seed all nations blessed

verses.text: And the angel of the LORD called to Abraham from heaven a second time,
saying, “By Myself I have sworn, declares the LORD, that because you have done this and have not withheld your only son,
I will surely bless you, and I will multiply your descendants like the stars in the sky and the sand on the seashore. Your descendants will possess the gates of their enemies.
And through your offspring all nations of the earth will be blessed, because you have obeyed My voice.”
Abraham went back to his servants, and they got up and set out together for Beersheba. And Abraham settled in Beersheba.
Some time later, Abraham was told, “Milcah has also borne sons to your brother Nahor:
Uz the firstborn, his brother Buz, Kemuel (the father of Aram),
Chesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel.”
And Bethuel became the father of Rebekah. Milcah bore these eight sons to Abraham’s brother Nahor.
Moreover, Nahor’s concubine, whose name was Reumah, bore Tebah, Gaham, Tahash, and Maacah.

tested.gloss: To test, try, or prove. A test in Scripture is not God gathering information He lacks, but a trial that reveals and refines what is truly in a person's heart. God tests Abraham to bring his faith to full expression, not to trip him up, and the test proves his trust real.

burntoffering.gloss: A whole burnt offering, from a root meaning to go up, as the smoke ascends to God. Unlike other sacrifices, the whole animal is consumed, a total gift given wholly to God. The word frames the entire ordeal: Isaac is asked as an olah, and a ram is finally given up in his place.

provide.gloss: To see, and by extension to see to, to provide. Abraham says God will see to the lamb, and names the place The LORD Will See, or Provide. Divine provision here is God seeing the need in advance and supplying it Himself, the same word behind the saying, on the mountain of the LORD it will be provided.

fear.gloss: To fear, reverence, or stand in awe. The fear of God is not cringing terror but a reverent trust that holds nothing back from Him. The angel says Abraham has shown this fear by not withholding his son, proving a heart that treasures God above even the dearest gift.

basic.context: After years of waiting, God gave Abraham the promised son, Isaac. Now God tells him to offer that very son as a sacrifice. Abraham obeys, step by agonising step, until God stops his hand and provides a ram instead. The key question is whether Abraham will trust and fear God even when God seems to ask for the promise back.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real event, told to reveal Abraham's tested faith and God's provision, not a command for anyone to imitate. God never intended Isaac to die, and He forbids human sacrifice elsewhere. The question the chapter presses is what genuine, God fearing trust looks like when everything is on the line.
basic.originalLanguages: God tests, nasah, Abraham, to prove and refine his faith. Isaac is asked as an olah, a whole burnt offering given entirely to God. Abraham says God will provide, raah, literally see to, the lamb, and names the mountain The LORD Will Provide. The angel says Abraham fears, yare, God, a reverent trust that holds nothing back.
basic.typology: The parallels to Christ are striking: a beloved, only son, carrying the wood up a mountain in the region later called Moriah, a father who does not spare his son, and a substitute who dies in the son's place. The ram caught in the thicket foreshadows the Lamb God would provide at the cross.
basic.christ: Where Abraham's hand was stayed, God did not spare His own Son but gave Him up for us all. Isaac carried the wood; Jesus carried His cross. The saying on the mountain of the LORD it will be provided finds its answer at Calvary, near Moriah, where God provides the true Lamb.
basic.god: God is the tester who refines faith, and the provider who supplies what He demands. He never wanted Isaac dead; He wanted Abraham's whole heart, and then He Himself gave the substitute. He is faithful to His oath, swearing by Himself to bless the world.
basic.crossReferences: John 3:16 speaks of God giving His only Son. Romans 8:32 says God did not spare His own Son. Hebrews 11 says Abraham reckoned God able to raise the dead. James 2 points to Abraham's faith proven by works.
basic.oneStory: The promise nearly dies on the altar, then God provides a substitute and renews His oath to bless all nations through Abraham's seed. That seed is Christ, and the mountain of provision points straight to the cross where the true Lamb is given.

context.literary: The dramatic peak of the Abraham narrative, structured by repeated calls and the refrain Here I am. Tension builds through spare, unhurried detail, then breaks at the angel's cry; the chapter closes by renewing the covenant oath, tying the test back to the promises of chapter 12.
context.historical: Child sacrifice was practised by some surrounding peoples, which makes God's command shocking and His refusal of it decisive. Moriah is later associated with Jerusalem and the temple mount, 2 Chronicles 3:1, grounding the scene in the geography of Israel's worship.
context.canonical: The passage becomes a touchstone for faith, Hebrews 11, and for justification proven by obedience, James 2. Its imagery of the beloved son, the provided lamb, and the father who does not withhold echoes through the New Testament's account of the cross.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Abraham's faith brought to its fullest proof, to reveal God as the one who both tests and provides, and to renew and deepen the covenant oath through the obedience of the man of promise.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a unique, once for all test of Abraham; it does not prescribe human sacrifice, which God explicitly halts and elsewhere condemns. The enduring lessons are wholehearted trust, the fear of God, and confidence in God's provision.

debates.question: In what sense does God test Abraham, given that God already knows his heart?

views.label: The test reveals and proves
views.case: God's foreknowledge is not in question; the test brings Abraham's faith into the open and demonstrates it, so that now I know means the trust has been fully expressed and proven in action.

views.label: The test forms and matures
views.case: The trial is not only demonstration but formation, deepening Abraham's faith and drawing it to a new height, so that he learns for himself that God provides.

debates.bearing: Either way the point stands: the test is for Abraham's good and God's glory, not a probe by an ignorant God, and it ends not in death but in provision and renewed promise.

study.originalLanguages: Key Hebrew words carry the drama. God tests, nasah, to prove Abraham's faith. Isaac is requested as an olah, a whole burnt offering wholly surrendered to God. At the heart of the story is raah, to see or provide: Abraham trusts that God will see to the lamb, and names the mountain The LORD Will See. The angel commends Abraham's fear, yare, of God, a reverent trust shown by withholding nothing, even his only son.

typology.type: The beloved, only son offered by his father
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the beloved Son whom the Father did not spare but gave up for us all, Romans 8:32.

typology.type: Isaac carrying the wood up the mountain
typology.fulfillment: Christ carrying His cross to Golgotha, near the region of Moriah, John 19:17.

typology.type: The ram provided in place of the son
typology.fulfillment: The Lamb of God, provided by God, who dies in the place of sinners, John 1:29.

typology.type: The LORD Will Provide on the mountain
typology.fulfillment: God's provision of atonement at the cross, on the mountain of the LORD, once for all.

study.christ: The chapter is one of the clearest foreshadowings of the cross in the Old Testament. A father is asked to give up his only, beloved son as a whole burnt offering; the son carries the wood up a mountain in the land of Moriah, later tied to Jerusalem. Abraham's hand is stayed and a ram is provided as a substitute, but at Calvary God did not spare His own Son. Abraham said God will provide the lamb, and John points to Jesus as the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world. The saying, on the mountain of the LORD it will be provided, is answered at the cross.
study.god: God both tests and provides. He tests Abraham to bring his faith to full proof, never intending Isaac to die and forbidding human sacrifice elsewhere. He is the God who sees to the need Himself, supplying the ram, and He is faithful to His word, swearing by Himself to bless the world through Abraham's seed. His demand and His provision meet in the same God.

crossReferences.note: For God so loved the world that He gave His only Son, echoing the only son whom you love.
God did not spare His own Son but gave Him up for us all, the reverse of Isaac being spared.
By faith Abraham offered Isaac, reckoning that God was able to raise him from the dead.
Abraham's faith was proven and completed by his obedience in offering Isaac.
Solomon built the temple on Mount Moriah, linking the place of the test to the place of Israel's worship.

study.oneStory: The son of promise is laid on the altar, and with him the whole future God had pledged; then God provides a substitute and swears again to bless every nation through Abraham's seed. That seed is Christ, and the mountain where God provided a ram points ahead to the mountain where God gave His own Son, so that the promise to bless the world runs straight from Moriah to Calvary.

quiz.q: What was the purpose of God's command to sacrifice Isaac?

options.text: To test and prove Abraham's faith, never intending Isaac actually to die.
options.note: Verse 1 says God tested Abraham, and He stops the sacrifice and provides a ram.

options.text: To bring Abraham's trust to its fullest expression through obedience.
options.note: True and closely related, though it slightly narrows the point that this was a test God controlled from the start.

options.text: Because God delights in human sacrifice like the surrounding nations.
options.note: The opposite is true; God halts the act, and Scripture condemns human sacrifice.

options.text: Because God had changed His mind about the promise through Isaac.
options.note: God renews the promise at the end; He never abandons it.

quiz.q: What did Abraham say when Isaac asked where the lamb was?

options.text: God Himself will provide the lamb for the burnt offering.
options.note: Abraham's words in verse 8 express trust that God will see to the sacrifice.

options.text: That they would find an animal along the way.
options.note: He trusts in provision, but he names God as the provider, not chance.

options.text: That Isaac himself was to be the offering.
options.note: Though this was the command, Abraham's answer points to God's provision instead.

options.text: That there was no need for a lamb at all.
options.note: Abraham fully expects a sacrifice; he trusts God to supply it.

quiz.q: What was provided in Isaac's place?

options.text: A ram caught by its horns in a thicket, offered as a burnt offering.
options.note: Verse 13 records the ram given as a substitute for Isaac.

options.text: A lamb sent down from heaven.
options.note: A substitute is provided, but it is a ram caught in a thicket, not a lamb from the sky.

options.text: One of Abraham's own flock that he had brought.
options.note: The ram was provided by God on the spot, not brought from Abraham's flock.

options.text: Nothing; Isaac was released with no sacrifice made.
options.note: A real sacrifice was made, the ram in Isaac's place.

quiz.q: How does this chapter point forward to Christ?

options.text: A father gives up his only, beloved son, who carries the wood, and God provides the substitute lamb.
options.note: The pattern of beloved son, wood, and provided lamb points to the cross.

options.text: It shows that faithful people will always be spared suffering.
options.note: This misreads the comfort; at the cross the Son was not spared, which is the deeper point.

options.text: It teaches that God tests everyone in exactly this way.
options.note: The test is unique to Abraham; the enduring pattern is the provided substitute, not identical tests.

options.text: It proves that God ultimately requires no sacrifice for sin.
options.note: The chapter centres on a substitute dying, pointing to the sacrifice of Christ.

quiz.q: What does the angel commend in Abraham?

options.text: That he fears God, shown by not withholding his only son.
options.note: Verse 12 names the fear of God, a reverent trust that holds nothing back.

options.text: That he was willing to obey even a hard command.
options.note: His obedience is real, but the text names it specifically as the fear of God.

options.text: That he argued God out of the command.
options.note: Abraham does not argue here; he obeys and trusts God to provide.

options.text: That he loved Isaac more than anything else.
options.note: The commendation is the reverse: he did not put even his beloved son above God.

questions: God asked Abraham for the dearest thing he had. Is there something you are holding so tightly that it has taken God's place in your heart?
Abraham obeyed step by step, trusting God with the outcome he could not see. Where is God calling you to obey before you understand how it will turn out?
Abraham said God Himself will provide, and God did. How have you seen God provide in your own story, and how does that steady you now?
At the cross God did not spare His own Son for you. How does the depth of that gift change the way you face your own costly obedience?
The place was named The LORD Will Provide. What situation do you need to bring to God, trusting Him to see to it in His way and time?

translationNotes.note: The land of Moriah is later associated with the temple mount in Jerusalem, 2 Chronicles 3:1, linking this mountain of sacrifice to the place of Israel's worship.
The name Abraham gives, YHWH Yireh, means The LORD Will See or The LORD Will Provide; the Hebrew verb for see and provide is the same, tying Abraham's trust in verse 8 to the naming here.
